channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: RFK Jr. Is Driving a Vast Inquiry Into Vaccines, Despite His Public Silence
item.description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has toned down his public criticism of vaccines, under orders from the White House. But inside his department, a sprawling research effort is a top priority.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Trump Proposes Suspending Federal Gas Tax
item.description: Also, Kennedy is pushing a vast inquiry into vaccines. Here’s the latest at the end of Monday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:description: A gas station in Pasadena, Calif. today.

item.title: Supreme Court Continues Access to Abortion Pill by Mail, for a Few Days
item.description: Justice Samuel A. Alito Jr. has paused until at least Thursday a federal appeals court ruling against the Food and Drug Administration that would restrict access by mail to mifepristone.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The justices acted in response to a request from two mifepristone manufacturers that asked the Supreme Court to intervene this month after the conservative U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit restricted access to the pills.

item.title: Reflecting Pool Repairs to Cost $13.1 Million. Trump Had Promised $1.8 Million.
item.description: The Interior Department added $6.2 million to the no-bid contract for repairs to the Lincoln Memorial pool late last week.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Atlantic Industrial Coatings was hired to repair leaking joints between the pool’s concrete slabs, waterproof the pool’s bottom and paint it a shade called “American flag blue.”

item.title: Senate G.O.P. Stands by Security Money for Trump’s Ballroom
item.description: Democrats are trying to stoke public opposition to the project as they take aim at legislation to provide nearly $72 billion for the administration’s immigration crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: Construction continued on the East Wing last month.

item.title: Trump Administration to Scrap Rule Encouraging Conservation
item.description: The Biden-era measure was intended to protect millions of acres from industrial development and the effects of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Daniel Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: The repeal of the Biden-era rule affects roughly 245 million acres of public lands, mostly in the American West, about one-tenth of the country.

item.title: Pressure on Starmer Mounts as Dozens of Labour Lawmakers Call on Him to Quit
item.description: The chances of a leadership challenge against Prime Minister Keir Starmer appeared to rise sharply on Monday as several government aides announced their resignations.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer in London on Monday. “I get it. I feel it. And I take responsibility,” he said of last week’s results.

item.title: Trump Proposes Suspending Federal Gas Tax Until Prices Fall
item.description: Congress would have to agree to any suspension, and dropping federal taxes would not make up for the steep increase in prices since the war began.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices have risen about 50 percent since the United States and Israel began the war with Iran on Feb. 28.

item.title: The Voting Rights Decision Might Silence Black People in Fayette County, Tennessee
item.description: Black residents, who make up 25 percent of Fayette County, Tenn., won a new electoral map last year to change an all-white board of commissioners. They worry the Supreme Court’s ruling could threaten that.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Rory Doyle for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I’m just wondering, how long is it going to take Fayette County to pounce on the ruling?” said Wendell Wainwright, 75, who has lived there all his life.

item.title: Virginia Officials Ask Supreme Court to Restore Voting Map Drawn by Democrats
item.description: State officials asked the justices to overturn a Virginia Supreme Court decision that struck down a congressional map, a major defeat for Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Campbell Robertson
item.media:description: The Virginia Supreme Court ruled last week that Democratic state lawmakers violated Virginia’s Constitution in enacting a voting map aimed at increasing the number of Democrats in U.S. House seats.

item.title: Why Republicans Are Still Drawing House Maps, While Democrats Are Stuck
item.description: The G.O.P. has seized an edge on redistricting thanks to two court rulings, and it has more room to maneuver for extra seats before the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Steve Cohen of Tennessee and State Senator Raumesh Akbari, both Democrats, discussing their state’s new House map, which is meant to unseat Mr. Cohen.

item.title: Drilling Into the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica
item.description: Ten people. Eight weeks. Three thousand feet to pierce a fast-melting Antarctic glacier.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Chang W. Lee
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Drilling equipment on the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica in January.

item.title: Gisèle Pelicot’s Memoir Said Something Original and Taboo About Victimhood
item.description: A book by the world’s most famous survivor of sexual violence has been read as a manifesto or a cry of pain. What she wrote is far more complicated.
item.dc:creator: Parul Sehgal

item.title: Israel Prepares to Prosecute First Suspects in Oct. 7 Attack Led by Hamas
item.description: Parliament passed a new law that paves the way for military trials for hundreds of Palestinians suspected of taking part in the 2023 attack that ignited the two-year war in Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Johnatan Reiss
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: Palestinians wave their national flag and celebrate by a destroyed Israeli tank in southern Gaza on Oct. 7, 2023.

item.title: Former Hostage Calls on All of Israel’s Government to Resign
item.description: Rom Braslavski, who said he had endured starvation, torture and sexual abuse during two-year captivity, demanded that members “take responsibility and get out of our lives.”
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: Rom Braslavski, in glasses, with three other former hostages during a ceremony in Tel Aviv in January marking the return of Israel’s last remaining hostage.

item.title: How the U.S. Is Trying to Ensure the Dollar’s Dominance During Economic Turmoil
item.description: As the government has been devising plans to keep the dollar dominant, China has been making its own moves to increase global influence of the renminbi.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has been leading the talks over the currency swaps.

item.title: Trump to Be Joined by Elon Musk and Other CEOs at Xi Summit in China
item.description: The delegation includes business leaders across a wide range of industries, including Tim Cook of Apple and Elon Musk of Tesla.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager

item.title: Putin Said the War ‘Is Coming to a Close,’ but That’s Not All He Said
item.description: The Russian leader is walking a tightrope: acknowledging public discontent while offering no indication that he might give up on his demands.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin during the celebration on Saturday in Moscow of the Soviet victory over Nazi Germany.

item.title: Ukraine Looks to Step Away From Trump and U.S.
item.description: With peace talks on ice and Ukraine now more self-reliant, President Volodymyr Zelensky seems to be stepping away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Kim Barker, Constant Méheut and Maria Varenikova
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine in Yerevan, Armenia, last week. Mr. Zelensky has publicly criticized the United States in ways that would have been unthinkable last year.

item.title: Former Top Zelensky Aide Is Accused in Corruption Inquiry
item.description: Ukrainian news outlets named the suspect as Andriy Yermak, who resigned last year amid a sweeping scandal.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: Andriy Yermak resigned in November after law enforcement agencies searched his apartment as part of a corruption investigation, but he was not charged.

item.title: In This Nazi-Era Restitution Dispute, the Focus Turns to a Missing Cow
item.description: A family is vying for the return of a painting it thought was by Rubens. But an expert says it’s a copy because it does not include the artist’s depiction of a peeing cow.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Hickley

item.title: The Silence That Meets the Rape of Palestinians
item.description: Male and female Palestinians describe brutal sexual abuse at the hands of Israel’s prison guards, soldiers, settlers and interrogators.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Samar Hazboun for The New York Times
item.media:description: Suhaib Abualkebash.

item.title: We’re Thinking About Mental Health Diagnoses All Wrong
item.description: Neither genetics nor brain scans can distinguish a person with depression, A.D.H.D. or autism from one without.
item.dc:creator: Awais Aftab
item.category: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (Book)

item.title: Byron Allen to Buy a Controlling Stake in BuzzFeed
item.description: Jonah Peretti, BuzzFeed’s co-founder, will step down as chief executive and serve as its president of A.I.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Bethany Mollenkof for The New York Times
item.media:description: A company associated with Byron Allen, shown in 2019, will pay $120 million for a 52 percent stake in BuzzFeed.

item.title: Why Two Big Companies Just Cut Paid Family Leave
item.description: Deloitte and Zoom are among the employers reducing support for working parents, signaling a retreat from family-friendly benefits.
item.dc:creator: Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: Jonah Markowitz for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. is the only industrialized nation not to have paid family leave as a federal benefit.

item.title: First-Time Home Buyers Are Staying on the Sidelines as Costs Rise
item.description: The effects of the war in Iran have raised mortgage rates and lowered consumer confidence, making it even harder for house hunters.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sebastian Siadecki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lillian Rouse and her fiancé, Mark Debney, in their rental home in North Carolina. Ms. Rouse said that delaying a home purchase had also meant putting off starting a family.

item.title: U.S. Passengers Exposed to Hantavirus Begin Quarantine, Including One With Positive Test
item.description: Eighteen people from a cruise ship that faced an outbreak are being monitored at medical facilities in Nebraska and Georgia. One tested positive for the Andes virus, health officials said.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao, Jacey Fortin and Jin Yu Young
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Passengers being evacuated from the MV Hondius on Sunday.
